title: When the Movement Becomes Another Hierarchy
dek: *The workshop leader who shames you for not meditating correctly. The activist circle where your grief isn't radical enough. The yoga studio where flexibility becomes moral currency. When the structures meant to liberate us become new cages, your body knows before your mind does.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Spiritual and social justice movements often recreate the same power structures they claim to dismantle, turning healing into competitive performance. Hierarchies emerge wherever humans gather, but awareness of this pattern lets you participate without losing your voice. Your anger at these dynamics is information, not evidence that you've failed the path.

You joined the movement because something in you said yes. Maybe it was the poetry, the promise of collective care, the first time someone named what you'd felt in your chest for years. You showed up with your whole heart, and for a while it felt like coming home. Then you noticed the subtle rankings. Who gets to speak first. Whose trauma counts. Whose anger is righteous and whose is toxic. The same power games you fled, now wearing prayer beads and land acknowledgments. Your rage at this isn't a character flaw. It's your nervous system registering incongruence, the gap between stated values and lived behavior. In our experience, this specific flavor of anger carries diagnostic precision. It points to the place where ideology and embodiment split apart. ## The Predictable Arc of Every Liberation Project Social movements follow a pattern so reliable you could set your watch by it. A group coalesces around shared pain or vision. Early days feel electric, boundary-less, possible. Everyone's voice matters. Then the group grows. Resources become finite. Decisions need making. And humans, being humans, start sorting themselves. Harriet Lerner observed in her work on anger and relationships that we unconsciously recreate familiar power dynamics even when we consciously reject them. The daughter of an authoritarian father becomes the facilitator who can't tolerate dissent. The person who suffered religious shaming builds a wellness brand that pathologizes rest. We export our unmetabolized patterns into every structure we touch. This isn't cynicism. It's pattern recognition. Over the years I've seen meditation centers implode over who sits closest to the teacher, mutual aid networks fracture along lines of who deserves help, and anti-racism workshops become spaces where white fragility and performative wokeness create a new kind of silence. The content changes. The architecture repeats. Beverly Engel's research on emotional abuse helps explain why movements feel so destabilizing when they turn hierarchical. She notes that abuse in the name of healing creates a particular kind of confusion because the language of care becomes the vehicle for control. When someone tells you that your boundary is your ego, or that your exhaustion means you're not committed enough, they're using the grammar of liberation to reinstall dominance. ## The Body Knows Before the Mind Admits Your shoulders tighten during the community call. Your stomach clenches when you see the group chat notification. You notice you're holding your breath in spaces that are supposed to be about expansion. These aren't random sensations. They're your animal self registering threat in an environment that insists everything is safe. The skin prickles. The jaw sets. Your hands want to push something away but you've been told that resistance is just your conditioning, your privilege, your unwillingness to do the work. So you override the signal. You make yourself smaller. You perform the right words, the right postures, the right amount of visible growth. This is where [NOW Magnesium Malate (180 tablets, 2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B001F0R6UQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) becomes more than a supplement in my own practice. When you're chronically tensing against an environment that claims to be healing, your muscles pay the price. Magnesium supports the parasympathetic shift your body can't complete when you're simultaneously told to relax and to prove your commitment through perpetual labor. ## When Collective Care Becomes Competitive Performance The mutual aid network starts tracking who gives most. The meditation group starts whispering about who's truly awake. The activist space develops an informal ranking of whose oppression grants most authority. Suddenly you're not building the new world, you're auditioning for a role in someone else's hierarchy. Thomas Moore writes about the soul's need for genuine community while also warning against movements that demand you abandon your individual complexity for the group's coherence. Real collective care, he suggests, holds space for difference, for the parts of you that don't fit the narrative, for the anger that doesn't serve the brand. But when the movement becomes another hierarchy, difference becomes deviance. Your questions are reframed as resistance. Your exhaustion is evidence you're not doing your inner work. The structure needs your compliance more than it needs your wholeness, so it develops increasingly sophisticated ways to frame compliance as liberation. You can feel this shift in your throat. The words you don't say start piling up there, a thick silence that tastes like betrayal. Not their betrayal of you, though that's real. Your betrayal of yourself, the moment you decided the movement's approval mattered more than your own knowing. ## The Guru Doesn't Need a Title Hierarchies don't require formal roles. They emerge in the subtle distribution of attention, the patterns of who gets believed, whose experience becomes the template. The person who's been in the movement longest. The one whose trauma narrative is most legible. The charismatic speaker who can make oppression sound like poetry. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the question of where your authority lives. Not positional authority, but the bone-deep knowing that your experience is real, your perceptions valid, your anger informative. Movements that unconsciously recreate hierarchy work by destabilizing that internal authority, replacing it with external validation. The guru doesn't need robes or a throne. They just need your uncertainty. Your hunger to belong. Your unmetabolized shame that makes you willing to contort yourself for acceptance. And movements, even beautiful ones, can become machines that metabolize your self-doubt into their structural power. This isn't about demonizing leaders or rejecting all structure. It's about noticing when the structure starts serving itself more than the people it claims to serve. When protecting the movement's image matters more than addressing harm. When loyalty becomes more valued than truth-telling. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Reclaiming Your Knowing Find a place where you won't be interrupted. Sit or stand in a way that lets you feel your whole body. Take three breaths deep enough that you can feel your ribs expand. Now bring to mind a recent moment in the movement, the community, the space where something felt off. Don't analyze it yet. Just notice where in your body you feel the memory. Is it your chest, your gut, your throat, your hands? Place one hand on that spot. Say out loud, even if it's barely a whisper: "This is real. What I'm feeling is real." You're not deciding what it means yet. You're just acknowledging the signal. Take three more breaths. Notice if the sensation shifts, softens, intensifies. Then ask your body: "What do you need me to know?" Don't force an answer. Just listen. Sometimes it comes as an image, a word, a sensation of expansion or contraction. Write down whatever came, even if it seems trivial or confused. This practice isn't about achieving clarity. It's about rebuilding the pathway between your body's knowing and your conscious awareness, the pathway that gets severed when movements demand you override your instincts for the sake of belonging. ## The Anger You're Not Supposed to Feel There's a particular fury that comes when the space that promised liberation becomes another site of diminishment. It's compounded by the fact that you're often told this anger itself is the problem. That you're being triggered. That you need to do more work. That your rage is just your ego protecting itself from transformation. Maybe. Or maybe your anger is appropriate. Maybe it's the part of you that refuses to be gaslit, that knows the difference between accountability and control, between transformation and performance. [The CDC's guidance on stress and the body](https://www.cdc.gov/mentalhealth/stress-coping/cope-with-stress/index.html) confirms what your nervous system already knows: chronic environments where you can't trust your own perceptions create measurable physiological damage. Your anger at the movement's hierarchy isn't evidence that you're not evolved enough. It's evidence that you're paying attention. It's the voice that says: I came here to be free, not to trade one set of rules for another. I came to belong, not to audition. I came to heal, not to perform healing for an audience that's keeping score. This anger has work to do. Not the work of burning down every imperfect community, but the work of helping you discern where you can stay and where you need to leave. Where you can speak truth and where your truth will be weaponized against you. Where the hierarchy is acknowledged and negotiated versus where it's denied and therefore more insidious. ## Staying Without Betraying Yourself You don't have to leave every movement that develops hierarchy. You do have to stay awake. That means maintaining your connection to your own experience even when the group insists you're seeing it wrong. It means finding the people within the movement who can hold complexity, who don't need you to be perfectly aligned to value your presence. In my own practice, I've learned to track two things simultaneously: the genuine good the movement does and the genuine harm its hierarchy creates. Both are real. Holding both is adult consciousness. Collapsing into only one is the kind of splitting that movements exploit. Sometimes staying means participating differently. Showing up for the work but not the performance. Contributing your gifts without needing the validation. Keeping one foot out the door, not as lack of commitment but as protection of your sovereignty. You can be in community without being consumed by it. [Yogi Relaxed Mind (4-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B083T811XY?tag=spankyspinola-20) sits in my cabinet as a small ritual of this both-and awareness. Holy basil and other adaptogens don't make the hierarchy disappear, but they support your nervous system's capacity to stay regulated while you navigate complexity. You can care deeply and maintain boundaries. You can participate and protect yourself. ## What Gets Built After Disillusionment The movement failed you, or you failed the movement, or maybe both are true and neither is the whole story. You're standing in the ruins of what you thought would save you, and the anger is giving way to something more textured. Not bitterness, exactly. More like clarity purchased at a price you didn't want to pay. Here's what you know now that you didn't know before: liberation is not a place you arrive. It's not a community you join or a practice you perfect. It's a quality of attention you bring to each moment, including the moments when the structures meant to free you start to bind. You know that hierarchy will emerge wherever humans gather, because we're pattern-making creatures who sort and rank and organize. The question isn't whether to participate in human structures but how to participate without abandoning your knowing. How to contribute without collapsing. How to belong without betrayal. The tools we've gathered, [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) and [the assessments hub](/assessments), aren't about achieving some hierarchy-free utopia. They're about staying awake in the middle of inevitable human complexity. About trusting your anger as information. About building the musculature to hold both your longing for collective liberation and your commitment to your own integrity. ## Questions Readers Actually Ask ### Isn't criticizing the movement just dividing us when we need solidarity? Solidarity that requires your silence isn't solidarity, it's compliance. Real collective power emerges from people who can name what's true, including uncomfortable truths about group dynamics. The movements that survive and serve are the ones that can metabolize criticism without fracturing. Your honest noticing strengthens the work when it's received as information rather than betrayal. The question isn't whether to notice hierarchy but how to name it in ways that invite repair rather than defense. ### How do I know if my anger is legitimate or if I really am just being triggered? Both can be true simultaneously, and that's okay. Yes, your history shapes what activates you. And yes, the activation can be pointing to something real happening now. Instead of trying to determine which is "really" happening, get curious about what your anger is protecting. What boundary is being crossed? What value is being violated? Then you can address both the current situation and any historical wound that's strengthening your response. Check the patterns across [more articles in this journal](/articles) for frameworks that help distinguish between protective anger and displaced rage. ### What if I'm the one unconsciously creating hierarchy in my own community? The fact that you're asking means you're already doing the most important work: staying awake to your own impact. We all carry hierarchical conditioning. The question is whether you're willing to notice when it shows up and make different choices. Watch for moments when you need to be right more than you need to be connected. When you silence someone's perspective because it complicates your narrative. When you use your knowledge or experience as a cudgel rather than an offering. Hierarchy isn't something other people do. It's a pattern we're all swimming in, and your willingness to see it in yourself is how it begins to shift. --- The movement will keep moving with or without you. The question is whether you'll keep moving with or without yourself. Whether you can maintain the connection to your own knowing even when the collective insists you're wrong. Whether your anger can be information instead of evidence of failure. You don't have to choose between isolation and assimilation. There's a third path, harder and more interesting: participation with sovereignty. Showing up with your whole self, including the parts that don't fit. Contributing your gifts without needing the group to validate your worth. Staying long enough to do real work and leaving before you become unrecognizable to yourself. This is the practice we explore more deeply at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where the personal and political, the spiritual and structural, are always in conversation. Your anger at the movement's hierarchy isn't the end of your engagement. It's the beginning of a more honest one, where you participate as yourself rather than as the self the movement needs you to be. That's not betrayal. That's integrity. And it might be the most radical thing you can offer. *Tool for this work: [Source Naturals Theanine Serene with Holy Basil](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0014H3B3G?tag=spankyspinola-20).* ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on when the movement becomes another hierarchy, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
